info.name: PerspectiveGraph API
info.description:
All PerspectiveGraph endpoints: health checks, the scanner-webhook ingest API (:8081) and the GraphQL API (:8080).

Run the **Ingest** folder top-to-bottom to reproduce the demo (`make seed`): six core sources + two discovery collectors (Kubernetes, cloud network) that correlate into six ranked attack paths. The analyzer recomputes paths on its interval (default 30s), so give it a moment before querying GraphQL.

Adjust `apiUrl` / `ingestUrl` collection variables to point at another deployment.

Threat intel: run the backend with THREATINTEL=on to enrich CVEs with CISA KEV (exploited in the wild) + FIRST EPSS (30-day exploitation probability); the GraphQL queries below expose node.kev / node.epss and posture.kevOnPaths.

Auth: if the backend runs with INGEST_HMAC_SECRET / API_TOKENS set, fill the collection variables `ingestHmacSecret` and `apiToken` — a pre-request script then signs ingest requests (HMAC) and adds the bearer token to GraphQL requests automatically. Leave them empty for an open (dev) backend.

Multi-tenant: set the `tenant` collection variable (and use that tenant's `ingestHmacSecret`/`apiToken`) to scope ingest + queries to one tenant; the pre-request script adds the X-Tenant header automatically.

Action layer: drift alerts fire to ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL when a new path appears; per-path Falco/Sigma detections are on the GraphQL AttackPath; GET /export/ndjson is the SIEM enrichment feed.

exec: // Auto-sign ingest webhooks (HMAC) and authorize GraphQL (bearer) when the
// corresponding collection variables are set. Bearer covers GraphQL, exports

request.description: GraphQL/BFF server liveness.
Webhook receiver liveness.

Prometheus exposition: ingest/normalize/analyzer-pass/dead-letter counters + Go runtime. Scale metrics: perspectivegraph_analyzer_graph_{nodes,edges} (graph size), _analyzer_pathfind_seconds (parallel per-seed Dijkstra latency), _analyzer_snapshot_seconds + _analyzer_snapshots_total{mode="full|delta"} (incremental snapshotting). Always open, unthrottled.
Status of the agentless PULL connectors (e.g. aws): last run, events emitted, errors. Empty when CONNECTORS_ENABLED is unset.
PUBLIC (no auth): tells the dashboard login gate whether auth is required and, for SSO, the OIDC coordinates (issuer/clientId/authorizeUrl/tokenUrl/scopes) to run the Authorization-Code + PKCE flow.

item.name: Ingest — scanner webhooks
item.description: Same payloads and order as `make seed` (six sources). Re-posting is idempotent (deterministic node ids, upsert semantics).

request.description: Pre-normalized events: the cloud/runtime subgraph (load balancer, containers, IAM role, data stores) that scanner findings correlate against.
Trivy JSON report. The slug/pr/sha query params attach PR context so the action layer can comment on the originating pull request.
Which repository the image was built from: emits the Image --BUILT_FROM--> Repository edge that connects SAST findings to runtime workloads. Posted by a CI step right after the image push.
Semgrep JSON output: code weaknesses and exposed secrets, linked to the repository.
Cloud inventory/identity export: ALB, EC2, IAM role, S3 — adds the cloud attack surface.

body.raw:
[
  {
    "rule": "Terminal shell in container",

]

request.description: Runtime alerts. Marks the affected container, flipping every path through it to runtime-confirmed (ranked first).
Minimal template for hand-crafted events: any nodes/edges with the ontology labels. Edges whose endpoints are not in the graph yet are retried with backoff until the nodes arrive.
Raw `kubectl get … -o json`. Auto-discovers exposure topology: Ingress→Service→Pod→ServiceAccount→Role (internet → cluster-admin), with no hand-stitched ids.
AWS describe-* shapes (security groups / instances / VPC peerings). Auto-discovers reachability: internet-facing SGs + SG-to-SG → CONNECTS_TO (internet → web → PII db).

Posts the account's IAM reality. Emits CAN_ESCALATE_TO edges to a synthetic account-admin crown jewel; roles trusting Principal:* are marked internet_exposed. Read-only: needs only iam:GetAccountAuthorizationDetails.

body.raw:
{
  "_comment": "Supply-chain provenance for the payments-api image, as a CI step would assemble it from cosign + SLSA attestation + an SBOM (syft/trivy). The image is INTENTIONALLY unsigned (signed=false) with a low SLSA level: because it sits on the internet -> crown-jewel path, this trips the built-in 'no-internet-to-unsigned-image' invariant (an unsigned build is a tampering vector into a reachable workload). The SBOM lists the bill of materials as Library/Package nodes the image DEPENDS_ON.",
  "image": "payments-api:1.4.2",
  "signed": false,
  "slsa_level": 1,
  "provenance_builder": "github-actions",
  "source_repo": "payments-api",
  "slug": "acme/payments-api",
  "sbom": [
    { "name": "log4j-core", "version": "2.14.1", "type": "library", "purl": "pkg:maven/org.apache.logging.log4j/log4j-core@2.14.1" },
    { "name": "spring-core", "version": "5.3.18", "type": "library", "purl": "pkg:maven/org.springframework/spring-core@5.3.18" },
    { "name": "jackson-databind", "version": "2.13.0", "type": "library", "purl": "pkg:maven/com.fasterxml.jackson.core/jackson-databind@2.13.0" },
    { "name": "openssl", "version": "1.1.1k", "type": "application", "purl": "pkg:deb/debian/openssl@1.1.1k" }
  ]
}

request.description: Supply-chain trust + bill of materials for an image: signed (cosign), slsa_level (SLSA attestation), and an SBOM (plain component list OR a CycloneDX document from syft/trivy). An internet-reachable unsigned image trips the no-internet-to-unsigned-image policy invariant. Real use: cosign verify / cosign verify-attestation --type slsaprovenance / syft <image> -o cyclonedx-json.

Identity-federation from an external IdP (Okta/Entra/…): IdentityProvider(internet) --AUTHENTICATES--> User --ASSUMES--> IAM_Role. Federated role ARNs converge with the IAM collector, so a no-MFA user federating into an admin/escalation role lights up the modern path internet → SSO → user → cloud admin. Assemble from the Okta/Entra admin API.
A real classifier finding marks the asset a crown jewel (basis classified:<source>:<kind>). label defaults to Bucket; use Database for an RDS/SQL store.

Everything the dashboard polls: posture, ranked attack paths with kill chain + generated remediations, policy violations, and the full graph. Paths are computed by an in-process Dijkstra by default; set ANALYZER_DB_PATHS=true to compute them in-database (AGE Cypher, safe-railed, bounded by ANALYZER_MAX_HOPS).

graphql.query: { status { version passes paths analyzedAt prunedNodes prunedEdges lastPrunedAt } }

request.description: Cheap analysis fingerprint. Clients poll this every few seconds and only refetch the full dashboard when version/passes change, instead of re-pulling the whole graph.

graphql.query: { history(points: 200) { openPaths resolvedPaths mttrSeconds oldestOpenSince persistent trend { at criticalPaths riskPct } } attackPaths { id openForSeconds firstSeen reopens } }

request.description: The temporal layer: exposure trend over time, MTTR (mean time-to-remediate of resolved paths), how long each path has been open (openForSeconds), and regression flags (reopens). Set HISTORY_PATH to persist across restarts.

graphql.query: { attackPaths { id score scoreUpperBound correlatedHops confidence confidenceLabel steps { edgeType probability weightBasis weightConfidence } } }

request.description: Honest probabilities. Every path reports a confidence (high|medium|low) and every hop a weightBasis — kev|epss|runtime (observed evidence) vs cvss|severity|heuristic (estimate). It also exposes the independence assumption behind the score: score is the product of the hops (independent), scoreUpperBound is the weakest hop (the score if they share a common cause), and correlatedHops flags when ≥2 hops rest on the same basis — so the true exploitability lies in [score, scoreUpperBound] instead of a falsely-precise point.

item.name: Attack paths scoped to one application

graphql.query: { applications attackPaths(app: "acme/payments-api") { id score nodes { name } } graph(app: "acme/payments-api") { nodes { name label } } }

request.description: The graph is environment-wide; `app` scopes paths and the graph view to one application (repo slug or cloud `app` tag). `applications` lists the available identifiers. Paths are computed by an in-process Dijkstra by default; set ANALYZER_DB_PATHS=true to compute them in-database (AGE Cypher, safe-railed, bounded by ANALYZER_MAX_HOPS).

graphql.query: { remediationPlan { title kind pathCount coveragePct verification { verified pathsEliminated riskReductionPct removedEdges } } }

request.description: The fewest fixes that eliminate the most critical-path risk, ranked by a greedy choke-point set-cover. Each fix carries the ready-to-apply artifact (content) and the paths it cuts. Add (app: "...") to scope to one application.

graphql.query: { attackPaths { id detections { kind title filename content rationale } } }

request.description: Per-path Falco + Sigma rules that detect exploitation of the path's exposed workload (scoped by container/namespace, referencing the CVE + crown jewel).

graphql.query: { attackPaths { id score steps { edgeType from to probability attack { id name tactic } } nodes { name label internetExposed crownJewel } } }

request.description: After POST /ingest/iam, paths whose steps include a CAN_ESCALATE_TO edge are IAM privilege-escalation chains, e.g. internet → public-deployer (trust *) → CAN_ESCALATE_TO → account-admin (effective). Filter client-side on steps[].edgeType == "CAN_ESCALATE_TO".

graphql.query: { riskSimulation(iterations: 5000) { iterations anyCompromiseProbability anyCiLow anyCiHigh sensitivityLow sensitivityHigh expectedCompromised crownJewels { name label compromiseProbability ciLow ciHigh } } }

request.description: Per-trial edge realization -> P(crown jewel compromised) with a 95% Wilson CI, plus P(at least one compromised) and the expected count. Captures path multiplicity that the per-path product cannot. Reproducible per seed.

item.name: K-shortest paths to a crown jewel

graphql.query: { kShortestPaths(target: "customers-db (PII)", k: 5) { id score nodes { name label } steps { edgeType from to probability attack { id name tactic } } } }

request.description: Yen's algorithm: the top-k highest-probability routes to a crown jewel (target accepts a node id or name), best first - so cutting the single best edge doesn't hide the alternates.

item.name: What-if: cut an edge -> residual risk

graphql.query: { whatIf(cuts: [{from: "public-deployer", to: "account-admin (effective)", type: "CAN_ESCALATE_TO"}], iterations: 5000) { removedEdges riskReduction beforeRisk { anyCompromiseProbability } afterRisk { anyCompromiseProbability crownJewels { name compromiseProbability } } after { score nodes { name } } } }

request.description: Simulate cutting a set of edges (proposed remediation). from/to accept id or name; type is optional. Returns surviving paths and the quantified risk reduction, with common random numbers so the before/after delta is the cut's effect, not noise.

graphql.query: { posture { kevOnPaths } graph { nodes { name label kev epss } } }

request.description: CVE nodes enriched with CISA KEV (kev) and FIRST EPSS (epss), plus posture.kevOnPaths (distinct KEV CVEs on a critical path). Requires the backend running with THREATINTEL=on.

graphql.query: { attackPaths { id score runtimeConfirmed nodes { name label } steps { edgeType from to probability attack { id name tactic } } remediations { title kind filename rationale content } } }

request.description: Ranked paths (runtime-confirmed first). `remediations.content` holds the generated artifact (K8s NetworkPolicy / Terraform). Paths are computed by an in-process Dijkstra by default; set ANALYZER_DB_PATHS=true to compute them in-database (AGE Cypher, safe-railed, bounded by ANALYZER_MAX_HOPS).

graphql.query: { posture { nodes edges criticalPaths runtimeConfirmed policyViolations } }

request.description: High-level counters for the overview cards.

graphql.query: { invariantViolations { invariantId description severity nodes { id label name } } }

request.description: Architectural invariants currently broken (e.g. no-internet-to-crown-jewel).

graphql.query: { graph { nodes { id label name internetExposed crownJewel runtimeAlert severity cvss } edges { type from to probability attack { id tactic } } } }

request.description: The full node/edge view the graph canvas renders.

graphql.query: { search(query: "log4j", size: 10) { id label name score } }

request.description: Requires the optional OpenSearch index (make up-search + OPENSEARCH_URL). Returns [] when the index is disabled.
Lists every root query with its description — same metadata the GraphiQL Docs panel shows.

Board-level brief of the current posture, grounded in the live attack paths. Provider is server-configured: Anthropic (Claude, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) by default, or HuggingFace / any OpenAI-compatible endpoint (HF_TOKEN) when no Anthropic key is set. 503 when neither is configured (aiEnabled=false). Viewer role when auth is enabled. Audited as ai.summary.
Ask a question about your attack surface; answered from the tenant's real, capped attack-path context (the model summarizes your data, it doesn't invent assets). Audited as ai.query.
Plain-English walk-through of one attack path and the most effective remediation. pathId = attackPaths { id }. Audited as ai.explain.

One NDJSON record per asset on a critical path (on_critical_path, max_path_score, kev, runtime_confirmed, …) — the enrichment feed a SIEM (Splunk/Elastic/Sentinel) pulls to prioritize alerts. Tenant-scoped + auth-gated like GraphQL.

Renders the tenant's attack-path posture as a NIST OSCAL 1.1.2 assessment-results document: each path -> observation + risk, each undermined NIST 800-53 control -> not-satisfied finding. The artifact GRC tooling consumes.

Returns the Ed25519 public key (when EXPORT_SIGNING_KEY is set) consumers use to verify the detached X-PerspectiveGraph-Signature header on the OSCAL/SIEM exports. Open (the key is public); 404 when export signing is disabled.

item.name: Triage / suppression
item.description: Accept-risk / false-positive / mitigating-control / duplicate decisions that take a specific attack path off the active board, with an accountable owner and optional expiry. Keyed by attack-path id (from the GraphQL attackPaths query). Run order create → list → delete leaves the store clean.

body.raw:
{
  "pathId": "{{suppressPathId}}",
  "reason": "accept-risk",
  "owner": "secops@acme",
  "note": "Compensating WAF rule in place; revisit next quarter.",
  "ttlDays": 30
}

request.description: Take one attack path off the active board with an accountable triage decision. reason ∈ accept-risk | false-positive | mitigating-control | duplicate; owner is required; ttlDays (or expiresAt, RFC3339) is an optional auto-expiry after which the path returns to the board. Admin role when auth is enabled.
The tenant’s triage board, including lapsed (expired) decisions so they stay visible. Viewer role when auth is enabled.
Return a path to the active board (deletes its suppression). Idempotent. Admin role when auth is enabled.

item.name: Remediation ticketing
item.description: Closed-loop action: owned, tracked tickets per attack path. Run order create → list → close.

request.description: Open an owned, tracked remediation ticket for a path (idempotent per open path). pathId = attackPaths { id }; owner required. Admin role when auth is enabled. With TICKET_WEBHOOK_URL set, the ticket is also dispatched to an external tracker. Tip: copy the returned id into the ticketId collection variable to close it below.

exec: // Capture the created ticket id so '3. Close ticket' can use it.

request.description: The tenant’s remediation work board. Viewer role when auth is enabled.
Mark the remediation done. Set the ticketId variable from the create response first. Admin role when auth is enabled.

item.name: Open fix PR for a path

request.description: Open a branch + commit the generated fix (K8s NetworkPolicy / Terraform) + raise a pull request for an attack path. pathId = attackPaths { id }. Needs GITHUB_TOKEN (dry-run when unset). Admin role when auth is enabled.

item.name: Red-team / BAS validation
item.description: Ground the engine against reality: record whether paths are real (confirmed/refuted/partial) and what the engine missed, and read the precision/recall it implies. A BAS platform posts here; metrics also via GraphQL { validation { precision recall } }.

request.description: Record a red-team/BAS verdict on a path: outcome ∈ confirmed | refuted | partial (references a path id from attackPaths) | missed (a real path the engine did NOT surface — needs route, not pathId). source required. Admin role when auth is on. Feeds the precision/recall metric.
A real path the tester found that the engine did not surface — a false negative that lowers recall. No pathId; describe it in route.
All verdicts for the tenant plus the rolled-up metrics: precision = confirmed/(confirmed+refuted), recall = confirmed/(confirmed+missed) — over the tested subset, not a global claim.
